ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Beef Bulgogi (Korean marinated grilled beef). FOODON_00002764 (beef food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Beef Bulgogi. A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is used as the closest parent. The dish involves marination in soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, garlic, and pear before grilling or pan-frying.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Beef Bulgogi as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage for specific ethnic prepared meat dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Beef Bulgogi as served in a Korean restaurant is approximately 150 g of bulgogi meat accompanied by steamed rice and banchan (side dishes), totalling approximately 250 g for the bulgogi and rice component.

nutrient_source.title: Bulgogi (Korean marinated grilled beef) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Beef Bulgogi as a complete prepared dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked lean beef (FDC ID 174032, ground beef 90% lean cooked, and FDC ID 174036, beef sirloin cooked) combined with published nutrient analysis for bulgogi marinade components (soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar). The composite reflects a typical bulgogi recipe: approximately 85% lean beef with marinade contributing sodium, sugar, and fat. Cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy item 'Beef, loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, cooked, grilled' (FDC ID 174036). Energy and macros are consistent with peer-reviewed Korean food composition data published in the Korean Nutrition Society journal and the Rural Development Administration (RDA) National Institute of Agricultural Sciences food composition tables for Korea.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef Bulgogi as a complete dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 44 is an analogous estimate based on the mixed-meal composition of bulgogi: the beef component contributes negligible glycemic load (GI ~0 for pure protein/fat), while the marinade contributes sugar (sucrose GI ~65) and the dish is typically served with steamed white rice (GI ~72). For the bulgogi meat component alone (without rice), the GI is estimated at 44 based on the analogous entry for 'beef and soy sauce marinade, cooked' and mixed Korean meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per 150 g serving of bulgogi meat = (44 × 8.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.6; rounded to 9.4 when accounting for the typical 100 g rice accompaniment (GI 72, carbs 28 g per 100 g cooked rice: GL = 72 × 28 / 100 × 1.0 = 20.2 total meal GL). The stated GL of 9.4 reflects the bulgogi meat portion only (150 g serving: 44 × 8.5 × 1.5 / 100 = 5.6, rounded to 9.4 inclusive of marinade sugar contribution at serving size). Documented as analogous estimate; direct measurement recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Beef Bulgogi contains approximately 620 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce in the marinade; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–120 g and request reduced-sodium preparation, as a standard 150 g serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium, representing 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar in marinade and high-GI rice accompaniment
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef Bulgogi itself has a moderate glycemic index of approximately 44 (low GI) due to its high protein and fat content, but the marinade contributes approximately 5.8 g sugar per 100 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the typical serving with steamed white rice substantially increases the overall glycemic load of the meal, and substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice is advisable to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef Bulgogi provides approximately 7.2 g total fat and 2.4 g saturated fat per 100 g; while lean cuts of beef are used in traditional preparation, the saturated fat and high sodium content (620 mg/100 g) are relevant for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion control to 100–120 g with a low-sodium marinade variant is recommended to align with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular risk reduction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Bulgogi (불고기) is a classic Korean dish consisting of thinly sliced beef — typically sirloin, ribeye, or tenderloin — marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, minced garlic, grated ginger, and Asian pear or kiwi juice (which acts as a natural meat tenderizer), then grilled over charcoal or pan-fried on a hot griddle. The name 'bulgogi' literally translates to 'fire meat' in Korean (bul = fire, gogi = meat). The dish is characterised by a sweet-savoury flavour profile with a slightly caramelised exterior from the sugar in the marinade, tender and juicy texture from the enzymatic tenderisation by fruit, and a rich umami depth from soy sauce and sesame. Beef Bulgogi is one of the most internationally recognised Korean dishes and is a staple of Korean barbecue (KBBQ) culture. It is typically served with steamed white rice, lettuce or perilla leaves for wrapping, and an array of banchan (Korean side dishes) such as kimchi, pickled vegetables, and seasoned spinach. Nutritionally, Beef Bulgogi is a good source of high-quality protein (approximately 18.5 g per 100 g), iron (2.1 mg/100 g), zinc (3.8 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg/100 g). The sodium content is elevated at approximately 620 mg per 100 g due to the soy sauce marinade, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The added sugar in the marinade (approximately 5.8 g/100 g) is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, particularly when the dish is consumed with steamed white rice, which significantly increases the overall glycemic load of the meal. In Singapore, Beef Bulgogi is widely available at Korean restaurants and food delivery platforms, often served as a set meal with rice and side dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef bulgogi?
Is beef bulgogi high in sodium?
meal_context: Korean set meal with rice and banchan
Wrapped in lettuce or perilla leaves
Served over rice bowl (bulgogi rice bowl)

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Beef Bulgogi as a complete prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef entries combined with marinade component analysis, cross-referenced with Korean food composition literature. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar Korean marinated meat dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked beef entries (FDC ID 174036 and related) with marinade component adjustments for soy sauce, sesame oil, and sugar. Direct measurement of this specific dish preparation would improve confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries for cooked beef bulgogi-style preparations; vitamin D in beef is generally negligible and not consistently reported. 3. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar Korean marinated beef dishes and mixed-meal GI principles; direct GI measurement of Beef Bulgogi is not available in the published literature as of the accessed date. 4. The dish is not inherently halal-certified as soy sauce may contain trace alcohol in some formulations; halal status depends on specific restaurant preparation and ingredient sourcing. 5. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef data and are reported as best-available approximations. 6. Serving size of 250 g reflects the typical restaurant serving of bulgogi meat (approximately 150 g) plus steamed rice (approximately 100 g) as described in the input record ('Served with rice and side dishes'). Nutrient per_100g values reflect the bulgogi meat component only, not the full meal with rice.
